{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/475","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/475/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/475/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/475/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/475","id":2571112775,"node_id":"I_kwDOEV8z8M6ZQBFH","number":475,"title":"Allow the use of a combined function + Jacobian","user":{"login":"astrozot","id":2106594,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDY1OTQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2106594?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot","html_url":"https://github.com/astrozot","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-07T18:03:27Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T18:03:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I need to solve a relatively complicated function (which depends on changing parameters) several thousands or millions of times, and I am trying to obtain a code that is as fast as possible. I am able to provide the Jacobian, but I would like to be able to compute the gradient *and* the function itself within the same function to avoid repeating, costly computations. 

However, I do not see how one can use this package (and the sub package SimpleNonlinearSolve) with a setting where `f` and `df` share common computations. Ideally, I would like to be able to provide three separate functions: `f`, `df`, and `fdf`, with the last one used to compute at the same time the function and its Jacobian.

See also

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/specifying-analytical-jacobian-in-nonlinearproblem-a-la-optim-only-fg/115991

for a similar discussion.

P.S. For performance reasons, since my problem is in low dimensions I am using non-allocating functions and StaticArrays, so memoization is not an option.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/475/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/475/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788","id":566483635,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1NjY0ODM2MzU=","number":34788,"title":"Inconsistency between fieldnames and propertynames","user":{"login":"benninkrs","id":22162695,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyMTYyNjk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22162695?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs","html_url":"https://github.com/benninkrs","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/benninkrs/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2020-02-17T19:34:17Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T20:52:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, `fieldnames` returns the fields ascribed to a _type_, whereas `propertynames` returns the properties of an _instance_:
```
              _
   _       _ _(_)_     |  Documentation: https://docs.julialang.org
  (_)     | (_) (_)    |
   _ _   _| |_  __ _   |  Type "?" for help, "]?" for Pkg help.
  | | | | | | |/ _` |  |
  | | |_| | | | (_| |  |  Version 1.2.0 (2019-08-20)
 _/ |\__'_|_|_|\__'_|  |  Official https://julialang.org/ release
|__/                   |

julia> struct A
       x
       end

julia> a = A(1.0)
A(1.0)

julia> fieldnames(a)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching fieldnames(::A)

julia> propertynames(a)
(:x,)

julia> fieldnames(A)
(:x,)

julia> propertynames(A)
(:name, :super, :parameters, :types, :names, :instance, :layout, :size, :ninitialized, :uid, :abstract, :mutable, :hasfreetypevars, :isconcretetype, :isdispatchtuple, :isbitstype, :zeroinit, :isinlinealloc, Symbol("llvm::StructType"), Symbol("llvm::DIType"))
```
Since properties are abstractions of fields, I feel that fields and properties should be treated more consistently.  I have two independent suggestions:

1. Currently, the fallback for  `propertynames` is `propertynames(x) = fieldnames(typeof(x))`.  My suggestion would be to define the corresponding fallback `fieldnames(x) = fieldnames(typeof(x))` as well.  This seems to me to be the only sensible meaning when `x` is not a type.  Furthermore, this addition would be non-breaking.

2. IMHO, the result of `propertynames(A)` in the example above is unexpected.  I suggest defining the fallback `propertyname(x::DataType) = fieldnames(x)` (and related methods for special types such as `UnionAll` and `Tuple`).   I believe this would technically be a breaking change, but it would make the behavior of `propertynames(A)` consistent with `fieldnames(A)` and (arguably) more expected.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":0,"-1":2,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2397872007","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788#issuecomment-2397872007","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788","id":2397872007,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6O7J-H","user":{"login":"astrozot","id":2106594,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDY1OTQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2106594?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot","html_url":"https://github.com/astrozot","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-07T20:52:01Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T20:52:01Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I see this thread is quite old, but still I would like to add something to this discussion. The fact that `filenames` operates on types makes its possible to write efficient generated functions: for example, in `ConstructionBase` I see

```julia
@generated function check_patch_fields_exist(obj, patch)
    fnames = fieldnames(obj)
    pnames = fieldnames(patch)
    pnames ⊆ fnames ? :(nothing) : :(throw(ArgumentError($("Failed to assign fields $pnames to object with fields $fnames."))))
end
```

to check if a given `patch` (a `NamedTuple`) only contains fields of an object. This same code would not work with properties, and one is thus forced to use a much less efficient normal function.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2397872007/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```

julia> struct A
       x
       end

julia> a = A(1.0)
A(1.0)

julia> fieldnames(a)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching fieldnames(::A)

julia> propertynames(a)
(:x,)

julia> fieldnames(A)
(:x,)

julia> propertynames(A)
(:name, :super, :parameters, :types, :names, :instance, :layout, :size, :ninitialized, :uid, :abstract, :mutable, :hasfreetypevars, :isconcretetype, :isdispatchtuple, :isbitstype, :zeroinit, :isinlinealloc, Symbol("llvm::StructType"), Symbol("llvm::DIType"))
```
Since properties are abstractions of fields, I feel that fields and properties should be treated more consistently.  I have two independent suggestions:

1. Currently, the fallback for  `propertynames` is `propertynames(x) = fieldnames(typeof(x))`.  My suggestion would be to define the corresponding fallback `fieldnames(x) = fieldnames(typeof(x))` as well.  This seems to me to be the only sensible meaning when `x` is not a type.  Furthermore, this addition would be non-breaking.

2. IMHO, the result of `propertynames(A)` in the example above is unexpected.  I suggest defining the fallback `propertyname(x::DataType) = fieldnames(x)` (and related methods for special types such as `UnionAll` and `Tuple`).   I believe this would technically be a breaking change, but it would make the behavior of `propertynames(A)` consistent with `fieldnames(A)` and (arguably) more expected.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":0,"-1":2,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2399156854","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788#issuecomment-2399156854","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34788","id":2399156854,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6PADp2","user":{"login":"astrozot","id":2106594,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDY1OTQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2106594?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot","html_url":"https://github.com/astrozot","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/astrozot/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-08T08:12:17Z","updated_at":"2024-10-08T08:12:17Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@vtjnash thank you for your contribution. I do not know `Base.diff_names` (as it is not documented), but from what I see in the source code it is applied to Tuples rather than NamedTuples. In any case the use of `@assume_effects` is very interesting but is not enough to prevent allocations in general situations. For example

```julia-repl
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> @benchmark Base.diff_names((:a, :b), (:b, :c))
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1000 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  1.666 ns … 16.349 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     1.750 ns              ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   1.796 ns ±  0.186 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

         █      ▂                                             
  ▃▂▂▂▂▂▃██▄▄▃▂▂██▄▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▁▂▂▂▂▂▁▁▁▁▁▂▂▂▁▁▁▁▁▃▄▃▂▂▁▂▁▁▃▃ ▃
  1.67 ns        Histogram: frequency by time        2.13 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

In fact the "constants" `(:a, :b)` and `(:b, :c)` are factorized out by the compiler

```julia-repl
julia> code_typed() do 
       Base.diff_names((:a, :b), (:b, :c))
       end |> only
CodeInfo(
1 ─     return (:a,)
) => Tuple{Symbol}
```

However, in more typical uses the function is not so performant, and needs to allocate (as one would expect given the source code of `Base.diff_names`):

```julia-repl
julia> t1 = (:a, :b)
(:a, :b)

julia> t2 = (:b, :c)
(:b, :c)

julia> @benchmark Base.diff_names($t1, $t2)
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 891 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  126.223 ns …  84.414 μs  ┊ GC (min … max):  0.00% … 99.77%
 Time  (median):     138.086 ns               ┊ GC (median):     0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   167.713 ns ± 895.423 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  10.10% ±  2.96%

   ▁▂ █                                                          
  ▅██▅█▄█▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▂▆▂▅▄▂▄▂▂▂▁▁▁▁▂▂▁▁▂▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁ ▂
  126 ns           Histogram: frequency by time          248 ns <

 Memory estimate: 144 bytes, allocs estimate: 3.
```

So, going back to the original issue, it still seems to me that having `propertynames` operate on types instead of instances would be a nice thing.

To elaborate a little, I see two general use-cases of properties:

- One might have a `struct` with pre-computed fields in an internal constructor, useful to speed-up some calculations. Then I would want to keep track of the fact that some fields are pre-computed and do not have to be passed to the constructor. For example, to define a structure that represents rotations in the plane I could do

```julia
struct Rotation{T}
    α::T
    sinα::T
    cosα::T
    function Rotation{T}(α::T) where {T}
        s, c = sincos(α)
        new(α, s, c)
    end
end
Rotation(α::T) where {T} = Rotation{T}(α)

Base.propertynames(::Rotation, private::Bool=false) = private ? fieldnames(Rotation) : (:α,)
```

- In a somewhat opposite situation, one might want to have a `struct` with properties computed on-the-fly from the object fields. For example, a rectangular triangle could be defined as

```julia
struct Triangle{T}
    a::T
    b::T
end

Base.getproperty(t::Triangle, sym::Symbol) = sym === :c ? hypot(t.a, t.b) : getfield(t, sym)
```

The use of `propertynames` associated to an object instance might find a (very specific) use only in the second case. In all other situations properties could be assigned to types.

In any case, to avoid any breaking change, would it make sense to define a new concept helpful to distinguish the two different use-cases of properties? Something like `public_fields` for example?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2399156854/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
